content.text: *"If you love me, keep my commands."*{John 14:15}{An implication is the fundamental building block of mathematical reasoning.}{Alfred Tarski}

Simple propositions can be combined to form more complex statements using 
**logical connectives**. Just as grammar 
provides rules for combining words into sentences, logic provides rules 
for combining propositions into compound statements.

The simplest connective is **negation**, which simply 
reverses the truth value of a proposition.

content.title: Negation
content.content:
Let $p$ be a proposition. The **negation** of $p$, denoted $\lnot p$ 
(read "not $p$"), is the proposition that is true when $p$ is false, 
and false when $p$ is true.

content.title: Negation
content.problem:
Let $p$: "It is raining." Then $\lnot p$: "It is not raining."

If $p$ is true (it is indeed raining), then $\lnot p$ is false.

If $p$ is false (it is not raining), then $\lnot p$ is true.

content.title: Conjunction
content.content:
Let $p$ and $q$ be propositions. The **conjunction** 
of $p$ and $q$, denoted $p \land q$ (read "$p$ and $q$"), is true when 
*both* $p$ and $q$ are true, and false otherwise.

content.text:
The conjunction $p \land q$ requires both conjuncts to be true. If either 
is false, the whole conjunction is false.

content.title: Conjunction
content.problem:
Let $p$: "2 is even" and $q$: "3 is prime."

Then $p \land q$: "2 is even and 3 is prime."

Since both $p$ and $q$ are true, the conjunction $p \land q$ is true.

content.title: Disjunction
content.content:
Let $p$ and $q$ be propositions. The **disjunction** 
of $p$ and $q$, denoted $p \lor q$ (read "$p$ or $q$"), is true when 
*at least one* of $p$ or $q$ is true, and false only when both are false.

content.text:
This is the **inclusive or**: "$p$ or $q$ or 
both." In everyday speech, "or" is sometimes used exclusively 
("soup or salad" at a restaurant typically means one, not both), but 
in mathematics, "or" is always inclusive unless otherwise specified.

content.title: Disjunction
content.problem:
Let $p$: "Today is Monday" and $q$: "Today is Tuesday."

Then $p \lor q$: "Today is Monday or today is Tuesday."

If today is Monday, then $p \lor q$ is true (even though $q$ is false).

If today is Wednesday, then $p \lor q$ is false (both are false).

content.text:
The conditional is perhaps the most important connective in mathematics, 
as it underlies all theorems of the form "if… then…"

content.title: Conditional
content.content:
Let $p$ and $q$ be propositions. The **conditional** 
(or **implication**), denoted $p \limplies q$ (read "if $p$ then $q$" 
or "$p$ implies $q$"), is false only when $p$ is true and $q$ is false. 
In all other cases, it is true.

We call $p$ the **hypothesis** (or antecedent) and 
$q$ the **conclusion** (or consequent).

content.text:
The truth conditions for the conditional often confuse students. Consider: 
"If it rains, then the ground is wet." This promise is broken only if 
it rains and the ground is *not* wet. If it doesn't rain, the promise 
is not broken regardless of whether the ground is wet.

content.title: Understanding Conditionals
content.content:
Think of $p \limplies q$ as a promise. The promise is broken (false) only 
when the condition $p$ is met but the result $q$ is not delivered. If the 
condition is never met, the promise is vacuously kept.

content.title: Conditional
content.problem:
Consider the conditional: "If you score 90\% or above, then you get an A."

Let $p$: "You score 90\% or above" and $q$: "You get an A."

- If you score 95\% and get an A: $p \limplies q$ is **true**.

- If you score 95\% and don't get an A: $p \limplies q$ is **false** (promise broken!).

- If you score 80\%: $p \limplies q$ is **true** regardless of your grade (condition not met).

content.title: Biconditional
content.content:
Let $p$ and $q$ be propositions. The **biconditional**, 
denoted $p \liff q$ (read "$p$ if and only if $q$"), is true when $p$ 
and $q$ have the same truth value, and false when they differ.

content.text:
The biconditional is often abbreviated as "iff". It combines 
both directions of implication: $p \liff q$ means $(p \limplies q) \land 
(q \limplies p)$.

content.title: Biconditional
content.problem:
"An integer is even if and only if it is divisible by 2."

This is a true biconditional: every even integer is divisible by 2 (one 
direction), and every integer divisible by 2 is even (the other direction).

marginNotes.text: Let God be true, and every human being a liar.

Ask and it will be given to you; seek and 
you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you.
